title: FOMC Press Conference
summary: Warsh's first press conference as Chair: the FOMC held the funds rate at 3.5-3.75% but the message was a hard recommitment to price stability. He stressed that inflation has run above the 2% goal for more than five years, that 'inflation is a choice,' and that the committee is 'unambiguous and unanimous' that it 'will deliver price stability.' He stripped the statement down, dropped forward guidance as ill-suited to the conjuncture, and declined to submit a dot (the SEP median implied a hike, with nine participants seeing an increase by year-end). He dismissed the framing of a 'cruel choice' between jobs and inflation and judged current restrictiveness only 'uneven' (restrictive in housing, hard to call restrictive in financial markets), while repeatedly refusing to endorse either a near-term hike or cut. Net: hawkish in framing and commitment, but holding, with the hike kept explicitly on the table.

key_quotes.quote: I am pleased to report that members of the FOMC are unambiguous and unanimous. This committee will deliver price stability.
key_quotes.context: Recommitment to the 2% objective as the central message of the meeting.

key_quotes.quote: But the commitment to deliver is strong, unanimous, and unambiguous. And that's an important message we've missed for five years. And we're going to fix that.
key_quotes.context: Framing the multi-year overshoot as a credibility failure to be corrected — high inflation conviction.

key_quotes.quote: It's uneven. If I look at the housing markets as one example ... Fed policy appears to be somewhat restrictive. I would have a hard time managing to say those words if I were to see what's happening in financial markets.
key_quotes.context: He does NOT judge the stance to be clearly restrictive across the board — why the composite is not +4/+5.

key_quotes.quote: I don't believe that we have a cruel choice ... if we do our job, we can make strong growth, low prices, and strong employment mutually compatible.
key_quotes.context: Dismisses labor-side downside as a binding constraint on delivering price stability.
